stories.title: Trilogy Receives $64M Construction Loan for Tampa-Area Apartments
stories.excerpt: Trilogy Real Estate Group has received a $64 million construction loan for the development of The Albion, a 325-unit apartment community being built in Valrico, Fla. The project, located 14 miles east of downtown Tampa, is slated for delivery in the fourth quarter of 2028. Multihousing News reports the financing is in the form of a three-year, floating-rate loan provided by QuadReal Property Group. The Albion will be located at 104 South Mulrennan Road. The community will feature six mid-rise buildings on 13 acres. The community will offer a mix of one-, two- and three-bedroom units averaging 919 square feet. Planned amenities include a pool, a golf simulator, a speakeasy-style lounge and a fitness center. There will be coworking spaces, a pet park and spa, as well as bocce ball and sand volleyball courts. The JLL Capital Markets Debt Advisory team representing the borrower was led by Matthew Lawton, Jesse Wright, Kenny Cutler, JJ Hovenden and Miguel Pedersen. The post Trilogy Receives $64M Construction Loan for Tampa-Area Apartments appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Trilogy received $64M construction loan from QuadReal for 325-unit Valrico apartment community.
The Albion delivers Q4 2028 in suburban Tampa, 14 miles east of downtown.
Floating-rate three-year loan finances mid-rise development on 13 acres with premium amenities.
stories.summary: Trilogy Real Estate Group closed a $64 million floating-rate construction loan from QuadReal Property Group for The Albion, a 325-unit mid-rise apartment community in Valrico, Florida (14 miles east of downtown Tampa). The project, spanning 13 acres with one-, two-, and three-bedroom units averaging 919 square feet, is slated for delivery in Q4 2028. The three-year loan finances a community featuring high-end amenities including a speakeasy lounge, golf simulator, coworking spaces, and recreational courts.
stories.impact: Tampa's suburban eastward expansion continues to attract capital and construction. This deal signals developer confidence in Valrico's rental demand, likely driven by employer relocation to the broader Tampa MSA and outmigration from saturated urban cores. Floating-rate financing on a three-year construction timeline suggests lenders expect rate stability or decline; rising rates would pressure underwriting. Monitor Valrico absorption upon 2028 delivery against competing supply to gauge rent growth potential.

stories.title: Developer Duo Obtain Construction Financing for $147M Spelman/Morehouse Student Housing
stories.excerpt: Radnor Property Group and the Madrone Community Development Foundation have closed on financing needed for a $147 million, 305-unit, 793-bed student housing development in Atlanta. Multihousing News reports construction has begun, and the community is slated to be ready by fall of 2028 for students at Spelman and Morehouse colleges. The sale of approximately $146.8 million in tax-exempt and taxable bonds issued by the Development Authority of Fulton County was completed earlier this month. Morehouse and Spelman colleges entered into a 50-year lease agreement with Madrone as part of the development team for the project. The development team includes Radnor Property Group as lead development partner, Clark Construction and CD Moody Construction in a joint venture as construction manager, Moody Nolan as architect and Pape-Dawson as civil engineer. Raymond James, along with Loop Capital Markets, served as underwriter for the bond financing. Rents will be about $1,200 per bed per month for the students’ new digs. The post Developer Duo Obtain Construction Financing for $147M Spelman/Morehouse Student Housing appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Radnor/Madrone closed $147M tax-exempt bond financing for 305-unit, 793-bed student housing in Atlanta.
Spelman/Morehouse colleges executed 50-year lease; development delivers fall 2028 at ~$1,200/bed/month.
Construction underway; bond deal oversubscribed, signaling strong institutional appetite for student housing credit.
stories.summary: Radnor Property Group and Madrone Community Development Foundation closed $146.8 million in tax-exempt and taxable bonds issued by Fulton County Development Authority for a 305-unit, 793-bed student housing project serving Spelman and Morehouse colleges in Atlanta. The colleges signed a 50-year lease agreement, de-risking tenant credit. Construction is underway with delivery targeted for fall 2028. Rents are pegged at approximately $1,200 per bed per month. The bond sale was oversubscribed, indicating strong institutional investor appetite.
stories.impact: College-anchored student housing continues to attract institutional and tax-advantaged capital, especially when backed by long-term lease structures. This deal underscores the credit strength of HBCU demand and insulates developers from occupancy risk. For multifamily developers in adjacent Atlanta submarkets (Midtown, Downtown), the influx of 793 beds may compress local student rents and reduce addressable market, but signals broader Atlanta market health and access to capital.

stories.title: Protective Live, Berkadia Provide $50M for Two Senior Living Facilities
stories.excerpt: Senior Living Transformation Company (SLTC) and Sabal Investment Holdings have secured $50 million to acquire and improve two senior living communities that specialize in assisted living and memory care: Daylesford Crossing in Paoli, Pa., and the Villages of Windcrest in Fredericksburg, Texas. Protective Life provided debt on the Villages of Windcrest, while Berkadia financed the Daylesford Crossing deal, according to a release. No broker was listed on the transaction. SLTC and Sabal plan numerous capital expenditures at the two assets, including improving the delivery of technology and operations, and will work with Juniper Communities, an established provider that will manage both facilities. “These acquisitions showcase SLTC’s and Sabal’s ability to provide solutions for challenged sellers within this critically important real estate sector,” said Arnie Whitman, founder and managing partner of SLTC. Located at 1450 East Lancaster Avenue in Paoli, a town 20 miles northwest of Downtown Philadelphia, Daylesford Crossing consists of 78 units. Located at 702 West Windcrest Street in Fredericksburg, a town 78 miles west of Austin, the Villages of Windcrest is 81 units. Tal Seder, managing director of Sabal Investment Holdings, described the two assets as “quality, well-located properties with upside potential” in a statement and referenced the demographic tailwinds from the baby boom generation that began turning 80 years old in 2026. “The combination of a sizable, aging baby boomer population and years of limited new construction is bringing the industry to an inflection point,” Seder said. “Investment in this sector is both critical and ripe with opportunity.” Brian Pascus can be reached at bpascus@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: SLTC and Sabal secured $50M debt from Protective Life and Berkadia for two senior facilities.
Acquisitions: Daylesford Crossing (Paoli, Pa., 20 miles NW Philadelphia) and Villages of Windcrest (Fredericksburg, Texas).
Juniper Communities to operate both; capex focused on technology and operations improvements.
stories.summary: Senior Living Transformation Company and Sabal Investment Holdings secured $50 million in debt financing from Protective Life (Windcrest asset) and Berkadia (Daylesford Crossing) to acquire and upgrade two assisted living and memory care communities. Daylesford Crossing is located in Paoli, Pennsylvania, 20 miles northwest of Philadelphia; Villages of Windcrest is in Fredericksburg, Texas. Juniper Communities will provide management. Capital expenditures will focus on technology integration and operational improvements.
stories.impact: This is a senior living acquisition, not multifamily development, with limited direct relevance to apartment demand/supply. However, it signals that institutional capital (Protective, Berkadia) views distressed senior housing as a value-add opportunity, suggesting broader market dislocation in non-apartment housing and potential for overlapping labor/construction resources if senior facilities require capital-intensive repositioning. Monitor whether these funds become constrained or if lenders tighten senior housing terms, which could free capital for multifamily.

stories.title: Hines Trust Acquires Historic Charlotte Design Center
stories.excerpt: Hines Global Income Trust acquired the Design Center of the Carolinas from Asana Partners for $170 million. Once the Nebel Knitting Mill, Asana bought the various components of the historic development and turned them into offices, retail spaces and restaurants. Commercial Search reports that the set of buildings was built in the 1920’s and converted to office in 2008, with retail added in 2020. Asana purchased the original three Design Center buildings for $42.7 million in November 2016. Over the next several years, the firm added several nearby assets. The 238,733-square-foot Design Center is 88% leased. The asset totals 122,000 square feet of retail space and 117,000 square feet of creative office space. Paul Zarian and Hines’ Jesse Amundson represented the firm in the acquisition. Ryan Shore and Miles Theodore, with Eastdil Secured, represented Asana Partners. The post Hines Trust Acquires Historic Charlotte Design Center appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Hines Global Income Trust acquired Design Center of Carolinas from Asana Partners for $170M.
Historic 1920s mill converted to 238K-SF mixed-use (122K SF retail, 117K SF creative office); 88% leased.
Asana's 2016 purchase at $42.7M reflects significant value-add repositioning and appreciation.
stories.summary: Hines Global Income Trust acquired the Design Center of the Carolinas from Asana Partners for $170 million. The 238,733-square-foot mixed-use property, originally a 1920s Nebel Knitting Mill, was converted to office in 2008 and retail in 2020. It comprises 122,000 square feet of retail space and 117,000 square feet of creative office space, currently 88% leased. Asana had purchased the original three buildings in November 2016 for $42.7 million and added assets over subsequent years.
stories.impact: This is an office/retail conversion, not multifamily development. However, it illustrates successful adaptive reuse in Charlotte and validates mixed-use urban infill models. The $170M sale price (vs. Asana's $42.7M basis) signals strong investor appetite for stabilized mixed-use creative office assets. For multifamily developers, this underscores Charlotte's urban revitalization but suggests competitive pressure from office-to-retail and office-to-creative uses in historic districts, potentially limiting multifamily conversion pipelines.

stories.title: Tishman Speyer, Breakthrough Properties Provide $90M Refi for Life Sciences Campus
stories.excerpt: The life sciences boom isn’t finished yet. Gemdale USA has secured $90 million in mezzanine debt to refinance Aperture Del Mar, a 538,000-square-foot Class A life science campus that recently opened in San Diego, Calif. Tishman Speyer and Breakthrough Properties, its life science joint venture vehicle with bellco capital, provided the loan, which will refinance existing debt. Breakthrough’s investment is financed out of a life sciences fund, which held its first close in late 2025. No broker was listed on the transaction. PERE was the first to report the news. Randall Rothschild, global head of debt at Tishman Speyer Global, noted that Breakthrough Properties already has “deep life science experience” in the marketplace, as the joint venture has financed several San Diego-area life sciences assets. He cited the San Diego region as being one of the epicenters for the ongoing growth of the asset class. “We are pleased to provide a creative financing solution that supports the continued advancement of a best-in-class life science project in one of the country’s premier innovation markets,” said Rothschild. Located at 6040 Edgewood Bend Court in San Diego, Aperture Del Mar finished its first phase of construction in 2021 and began leasing in 2023. The life sciences campus is divided among four lab buildings and a separate office space and is 100 percent occupied by Neurocrine Biosciences, a leading biopharmaceutical company that will call the site its global headquarters through 2036. Aside from its five different buildings, the campus includes a fitness center, a yoga studio, an outdoor lawn for events, a private courtyard, and 1,500-space parking garage. “Aperture Del Mar offers a true Class A campus environment, and Neurocrine, with more than three decades of history in San Diego, is one of the region’s marquee biotechnology companies,” said Dan D’Orazi, chief investment officer of Breakthrough Properties. Since its 2019 founding, Breakthrough Properties has owned or
bullets: Tishman Speyer/Breakthrough Properties provided $90M mezzanine refi for Aperture Del Mar life sciences campus, San Diego.
538K-SF Class A facility recently opened; Breakthrough's dedicated life sciences fund reached first close in late 2025.
Deal underscores San Diego's status as premier life sciences market with strong institutional capital deployment.
stories.summary: Gemdale USA secured $90 million in mezzanine debt from Tishman Speyer and Breakthrough Properties (a life sciences joint venture with bellco capital) to refinance Aperture Del Mar, a 538,000-square-foot Class A life sciences campus that recently delivered in San Diego. Breakthrough Properties deployed capital from its dedicated life sciences fund, which held its first close in late 2025. The mezzanine structure and rapid refinancing suggest strong property stabilization and investor confidence.
stories.impact: Life sciences is a non-apartment asset class, but the deal signals institutional capital's conviction in San Diego as a bio-cluster and validates the region's job/economy growth. Strong life sciences real estate performance may compress multifamily development sites in San Diego's premium submarkets (Del Mar, La Jolla) as life sciences tenants bid up land and building values. Conversely, life sciences jobs drive high-income in-migration and sustained rental demand for Class A multifamily across San Diego County.

stories.title: Nomura Prices Largest Single-Bank SASB CMBS in Two Years
stories.excerpt: As it approaches its first birthday, Nomura Securities’ commercial real estate lending platform has an extra reason to celebrate: It just sealed the largest sole-bank single-asset, single-borrower commercial mortgage-backed securities (SASB CMBS) deal in almost two years. The $719 million KELR 2026-MF deal is backed by 12 multifamily properties and one student housing asset, all owned by Keller Investment Properties. Nomura is the sole lender in the floating-rate, interest-only deal, which has a two-year term with three one-year extension options. The deal marks not only the largest single-bank SASB since August 2024, but it also places it among the largest single-bank SASB deals since 2020 — not too shabby for a platform that launched only last summer. Further, the transaction received a strong response from investors and was multiple times oversubscribed, sources said. Newmark brokered the financing, and the AAA bonds — rated by Fitch Ratings and Moody’s — priced at 135 basis points over the Secured Overnight Financing Rate earlier this week. The portfolio collateral is spread across Utah, Nevada and Arizona, and totals 3,321 units, including the sole student housing property in the portfolio, which accommodates students at Utah Valley University. Keller acquired the portfolio between 2001 and 2022, and has since invested $46 million in capital improvements, according to Fitch’s presale report. The CMBS loan proceeds will refinance $696 million in existing debt on the portfolio and also fund $22.2 million in closing-related costs and reserves. The transaction may have been a first for Nomura, but it was also one for Keller Investment Properties, based just outside of Salt Lake City, which historically has been a borrower of agency debt, sources said. As such, KELR 2026-MF marks the company’s SASB CMBS debut. The deal closed in just over 60 days, which one source described as “a land speed record category for a first-time CMBS borrower.” “We are pleased with the ou
bullets: Nomura priced $719M single-bank SASB CMBS backed by 12 multifamily + 1 student housing property.
KELR 2026-MF deal is largest sole-bank SASB since August 2024; floating-rate, two-year term + extension options.
Portfolio owned by Keller Investment Properties; deal oversubscribed, signaling strong investor appetite for multifamily CMBS.
stories.summary: Nomura Securities priced the KELR 2026-MF commercial mortgage-backed securities deal, a $719 million single-bank, single-borrower SASB backed by a portfolio of 12 multifamily properties and one student housing asset owned by Keller Investment Properties. It is the largest sole-bank SASB since August 2024 and carries a floating-rate, interest-only structure with a two-year term and three one-year extension options. The deal was multiple times oversubscribed, indicating robust investor demand.
stories.impact: A strong, oversubscribed multifamily CMBS deal signals capital is flowing into stabilized multifamily portfolios and that lenders are comfortable with floating-rate structures despite rate uncertainty. For developers, this underscores competitive pressure from portfolio players (Keller) and validates the SASB market as a viable exit for stabilized, performing assets. Oversubscription also suggests cap rate compression may continue for Class A multifamily in core markets, tightening development spreads and pushing entitlements to edge markets.

stories.title: No, the Pfizer Near-Disaster Won’t Slow New York’s Conversion Wave
stories.excerpt: New York City is relying, in part, on more than 16,000 units planned via office-to-residential conversions to ease its enduring housing crisis. So, when the country’s largest conversion, sitting in the heart of Midtown, starts to buckle, it’s not taken lightly. The startling structural emergency at MetroLoft and David Werner Real Estate Investment’s Pfizer building conversion in early July prompted a swift response from the city. Reports of two buckling columns prompted officials to cordon off 40th to 45th streets between First and Third avenues in the immediate aftermath. Several buildings were evacuated, including a school where roughly 400 children were relocated. In the following weeks, after temporary supports secured the building, the New York City Department of Buildings (DOB) initiated citywide construction safety sweeps, including checks on projects linked to MetroLoft’s contractor, Barone Steel Fabricators, Gothamist reported. A full investigation of the emergency is ongoing, but the final verdict will likely come down to the two columns, and the heavy load above them. The real estate industry is largely in agreement that the Pfizer emergency was a freak accident. Yet, when a conversion of such fame and magnitude — in which 19 stories and 1,600 apartments are being added to the existing structure — causes such disruption, anxiety is understandable. New regulations from the city, and accompanying cost overruns, may now be the industry’s real worry. Is it enough to spook the market? Development, real estate and housing advocates sure hope not. Conversion projects, like any major development, experience pressures to stay on time and on budget, intensified by an inflationary, volatile capital markets environment and heightened construction costs in the Big Apple. Insiders argue that the scare is unlikely to derail New York’s office conversion boom, given the investor appetite and political will behind multifamily housing. New York City boasts the largest conve
bullets: MetroLoft/David Werner Pfizer conversion in Midtown Manhattan experienced structural emergency (buckling columns) in July 2026.
NYC relies on 16K+ units from office-to-residential conversions to address housing crisis; this high-profile failure triggered DOB safety sweeps.
Contractor Barone Steel Fabricators under investigation; full structural verdict pending.
stories.summary: The MetroLoft and David Werner Real Estate Investment Pfizer building office-to-residential conversion project in Midtown Manhattan (40th–45th Streets, First–Third Avenues) experienced a structural emergency in early July 2026 when two columns buckled, triggering evacuations of nearby buildings and schools. The building represents the largest office-to-residential conversion underway in the city. The incident prompted the NYC Department of Buildings to launch citywide construction safety sweeps, including audits of projects using contractor Barone Steel Fabricators. A full structural investigation is ongoing.
stories.impact: This high-profile structural failure directly threatens NYC's office-to-residential conversion pipeline—a critical supply lever for the city's housing crisis. If future DOB scrutiny slows permitting or increases construction costs (reinforced inspections, design modifications), conversion economics worsen dramatically, reducing the viability of marginal projects and delaying supply delivery. Developers bidding on conversion projects will face higher insurance, contingency, and structural review costs. This may push conversions toward renovated office stock with superior original design and structural margins, favoring newer postwar buildings over older Midtown stock.

stories.title: Inflation Could Stay Higher: What it Means for CRE
stories.excerpt: The June Consumer Price Index offered some good news: inflation dropped to 3.5%, driven by lower oil and gas prices. However, that state of affairs is unlikely to last, especially as tensions in the Middle East are heating up again, suggesting the U.S. isn’t free from the elevated inflation cycle just yet. In a recently released video, “Inflation Risk: Headwind or Opportunity for CRE?” Vice President John Chang discussed the realities of inflation and its potential impact on commercial real estate. First, the ongoing Middle East conflict could lead to an “extended cycle of elevated prices, which, in turn, could sustain inflationary pressure,” he said. It could also signal a higher probability of a federal interest rate hike by the end of the year. Second, other inflation metrics, like food, housing and medical care, have comparatively low inflation numbers in the 2 to mid-3% range. Energy prices, which affect transportation costs, could lead to ongoing higher headline inflation. Third, this isn’t unexpected news for commercial real estate investors. “In large part, the risk of potential rate increases is already baked into Treasury and lending rates, and because they’re already baked in, the cost of debt capital should remain relatively stable,” Chang said. Fourth, even in an extended inflation cycle, commercial real estate is one of the few investments that can offer some inflation resistance. “Despite elevated uncertainty, despite the Middle East conflict, and despite tariff-driven headwinds, all four of the major property types are generating positive space demand,” Chang said. Based on preliminary reports from Q2, office properties reported a ninth consecutive quarter of positive net absorption, while apartment demand pushed vacancy rates lower. At the same time, retail and industrial space had relatively stable vacancy rates. Finally, inflation could decline, and economic momentum could strengthen if a resolution to the Middle East conflict is reached. That cou
bullets: June CPI dropped to 3.5% but energy/Middle East tensions signal sustained inflation risk through year-end.
Food, housing, medical inflation remain low (2–3.5%), but transportation/energy costs could sustain headline inflation.
Rate increases already priced into Treasury and lending spreads; debt capital costs expected to remain stable.
stories.summary: June 2024 CPI fell to 3.5%, driven by lower energy prices, but Middle East geopolitical tensions suggest elevated inflation may persist. While core inflation in food, housing, and medical care remains low (2–3.5%), energy and transportation costs could sustain higher headline inflation and increase the risk of a federal rate hike by year-end. However, investors and lenders have already priced in rate risk, stabilizing debt capital costs.
stories.impact: Stable debt capital costs despite inflation uncertainty is favorable for multifamily construction financing, but sustained inflation pressures could increase material, labor, and carry costs on long-duration projects. Developers should lock in construction loan terms and material pricing; floating-rate construction financing becomes riskier if inflation re-accelerates and the Fed raises rates. Inflation also pressures renter purchasing power, particularly below-market-rate segments; monitor rent growth vs. wage growth in submarkets with significant household income under $50K.

stories.title: Office’s New Purpose: Affiliation
stories.excerpt: In 2025, global office utilization reported its largest annual gain in four years. But employees aren’t returning simply because they’re being told to. Lenny Beaudoin According to CBRE’s 2026 “Global Workplace & Occupancy Insights” survey report, organizations that design their office spaces for collaboration, culture and connection are experiencing the strongest attendance gains. Office utilization rose to 53% in 2025 from 38% a year earlier. Peak utilization reached 80%, while average occupancy climbed to 111%, reflecting a higher level of desk sharing and more flexible workplace strategies. “We’re seeing the best attendance gains where organizations make the office a more purposeful place to be,” Lenny Beaudoin, CBRE’s executive managing director of workplace, design and occupancy, told Connect CRE. Beaudoin, who co-authored the report with CBRE colleague Susan Wasmund, said organizations are increasingly measuring office performance by what the workplace supports rather than how full it is. “It’s being judged by what it enables,” he said. “The next generation of workplace strategy is about affiliation, collaboration, culture and performance.” Collaboration Replaces Solo Work The report suggests that the office’s primary purpose has shifted from providing individual work space to fostering interaction. Among survey respondents, 93% said that colleague collaboration was a main reason for coming into the office, followed by in-person meetings (90%) and social interaction and team building (89%). That shift is also impacting office layouts. Between 2021 and 2025, space dedicated to individual workstations fell from 56% to 35% of the workplace, while collaborative areas expanded from 12% to 56%. What about amenities? The report said that vacancy in prime office buildings that offer plentiful amenities is “measurable lower than the broader market.” More important than the latest, shinies
bullets: Global office utilization jumped to 53% in 2025 from 38% YoY; peak reached 80%, average occupancy 111%.
Organizations designing offices for collaboration, culture, connection see strongest attendance gains; affiliation, not occupancy density, is new metric.
Desk sharing and flexible workplace strategies now standard; next-gen office strategy tied to performance enablement.
stories.summary: CBRE's 2026 Global Workplace & Occupancy Insights report shows global office utilization surged to 53% in 2025 from 38% a year earlier, with peak utilization reaching 80% and average occupancy climbing to 111% (reflecting increased desk sharing). Organizations designing offices for collaboration, culture, and connection are experiencing the strongest attendance gains. The metric of success has shifted from how full an office is to what it enables—performance, affiliation, and culture rather than occupancy density.
stories.impact: While office-focused, this data is relevant to multifamily because it signals sustained corporate flexibility and potential distributed work adoption, reducing centralized office demand in core CBD submarkets. Developers betting on downtown revitalization should monitor whether growth is driven by residents and consumer spending or by office workers. Rising desk-sharing and flexible strategies may further weaken office demand, limiting conversion and urban infill economics. However, culture-driven 'third places' and collaborative spaces may drive mixed-use development and support ground-floor retail, which benefits adjacent residential.

stories.title: Forest Development, Kast Construction face off in legal battle over Lake Park condo
stories.excerpt: Forest Development and Kast Construction are going head-to-head in a legal battle over Nautilus 220 condominiums in Lake Park.  The condo’s developer and builder are suing each other for alleged contract breach, incomplete work and failure to pay millions of dollars owed, The Palm Beach Post reported.  Forest Development filed a lawsuit July 13 in Palm Beach County circuit court, accusing Kast of not completing work and alleging that the builder’s improper supervision of its subcontractors resulted in water damage and unnecessary delays. The lawsuit also claims Kast refused to release liens on sold properties, hindering Forest’s ability to close […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Forest Development and Kast Construction engaged in dueling lawsuits over Nautilus 220 condos in Lake Park, Florida.
Forest alleges Kast failed to complete work, caused water damage, withheld lien releases; Kast claims $11.4M in unpaid progress payments.
Subcontractors have filed $2.9M in additional liens; contractor disputes over change orders and design delays created workflow disruption.
stories.summary: Forest Development and Kast Construction are litigating over Nautilus 220 condominiums in Lake Park, Florida. Forest filed a lawsuit alleging Kast failed to complete work, caused water damage through poor subcontractor oversight, and refused to release liens on sold properties, blocking closings. Kast countersued, claiming Forest failed to make $11.4 million in monthly progress payments and submitted change orders it later refused to fund. Kast placed an $11.4 million lien on the project; subcontractors have filed additional $2.9 million in liens.
stories.impact: This is a condo project, not multifamily rental, but illustrates construction financing and contractor execution risk. Disputes over change orders, design delays, and payment flow are common multifamily pain points. Developers should enforce rigid change-order protocols, use tripartite payment agreements, and stress-test construction budgets; payment delays cascade to subcontractors and stall projects. For multifamily lenders, this case underscores importance of draw reviews and lien waivers before funding. South Florida's hot construction environment may be attracting less-experienced or undercapitalized contractors, increasing default risk.

stories.title: Policy Pro: Developers back ballot bid to speed up construction permits
stories.excerpt: This story gives you a peek at the content coming to our new platform, TRD Policy Pro. Sign up to get early access here. Hello, let’s get into today’s news at the intersection of policy and real estate: In this edition we mention: Gotham Organization President of Development Brian Kelly, Monadnock Development President Kirk Goodrich, Open New York Executive Director Annemarie Gray, the Department of Finance, the Department of Buildings and others. We Heard Have a tip or feedback? Reach me at caroline.spivack@therealdeal.com.  The Catch-Up Imposing new union-requested wage mandates on office-to-residential conversions would increase labor costs, making fewer projects […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: NYC developers backing ballot measure to accelerate construction permitting amid housing crisis.
Union wage mandates on office-to-residential conversions could increase labor costs, reduce project viability and housing supply.
NYC delays publishing Opportunity Zone information; Congress pushing HUD reforms but staffing cuts risk rollout delays.
stories.summary: NYC developers are supporting a ballot initiative to speed up construction permitting. However, proposed union wage mandates on office-to-residential conversions threaten project economics by raising labor costs, potentially rendering marginal projects financially unviable and reducing planned new housing supply. Additionally, New York has yet to publicize its next-iteration Opportunity Zone designations, lagging 14 other states and territories. Congress has tasked HUD with sweeping housing reforms, but deep staffing cuts may slow implementation.
stories.impact: Wage mandates on conversions significantly increase carrying costs and labor risk for NYC apartment developers relying on conversion economics. If enforced, they will reduce starts in marginal submarkets and push projects toward high-barrier, higher-rent neighborhoods. Conversion pipeline delays multiply if permitting accelerates but wage mandates cause prior projects to stall or are abandoned. Delayed OZ designation limits tax-advantaged equity for development; HUD staffing shortages could further delay grant programs and tax credit allocations essential for affordable housing. Developers should lobby for wage mandate carve-outs or federal subsidy offsets.

stories.title: Fashion Nova Expands L.A. Footprint With 133K-SF Warehouse Deal
stories.excerpt: Fashion Nova is expanding its Southern California logistics side with another industrial lease in Santa Fe Springs. The Los Angeles-based fast-fashion retailer signed a lease to take over the 132,888-square-foot warehouse at 12301 Hawkins Street, according to CoStar. It increases Fashion Nova’s warehouse footprint in Santa Fe Springs by about 33 percent. New York-based real estate investment manager Clarion Partners owns the property in the Heritage Springs Business Park. It includes 12,004 square feet of office space on a 5.4-acre site, according to a JLL marketing brochure. The recently renovated facility features 30-foot clear heights, 18 dock-high doors, two ground-level doors and 175 parking spaces. The owner did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The deal adds to leasing momentum picking up across L.A. County’s industrial market. Leasing activity reached 15.7 million square feet during the second quarter, up 40 percent compared to last year, according to CBRE. The market also posted 2.8 million square feet of positive net absorption — its third consecutive positive quarter after 10 straight quarters of negative absorption. Vacancy declined 22 basis points since the first quarter to 5 percent, although landlords continue to face falling rents. Average asking rents dropped to $1.19 per square foot per month, down 7 percent from a year prior, and down 32.4 percent from the second quarter of 2023. The Mid-Counties submarket, which includes Santa Fe Springs, recorded 1.1 million square feet of positive absorption during the quarter and an average asking rate of $1.23 per square foot. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Fashion Nova leased 132,888-SF warehouse in Santa Fe Springs, California; expands local footprint by ~33%.
Property owned by Clarion Partners; includes 12K SF office on 5.4 acres; renovated facility with 30-foot clear heights.
LA County industrial leasing reached 15.7M SF in Q2, up 40% YoY; positive net absorption continues.
stories.summary: Fashion Nova signed a lease for a 132,888-square-foot warehouse at 12301 Hawkins Street in Santa Fe Springs, California, increasing its local Santa Fe Springs footprint by approximately 33%. The recently renovated property, owned by Clarion Partners, sits in the Heritage Springs Business Park and includes 12,004 square feet of office space on 5.4 acres, featuring 30-foot clear heights, 18 dock-high doors, and 175 parking spaces. LA County's industrial leasing market posted 15.7 million square feet in Q2 2026, up 40% year-over-year.
stories.impact: This is industrial, not multifamily, but the data underscores strong logistics/e-commerce demand in LA County, driving capital and leasing activity. Tight industrial supply and rising logistics rents may compress multifamily development sites and labor availability in Santa Fe Springs and adjacent submarkets. However, strong industrial employment and logistics hub status support sustained in-migration and rental demand for workforce housing near industrial parks. Developers should prioritize worker-oriented multifamily (workforce/market-rate studios and one-bedrooms) near logistics clusters in LA County's outer suburbs.

stories.title: Mandarin Oriental Brickell Key, Live Local high-rise among $420M-plus South Florida construction permits for week ending July 22, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 New Building: The largest permit issued was a $240 million new construction permit for 1 Island Drive in Miami, where Swire Properties is developing The Residences at Mandarin Oriental, Miami and Mandarin Oriental Hotel on Brickell Key. The former Mandarin Oriental Hotel towers on the site were demolished via controlled implosion in April. The redevelopment will include condos and a hotel, totaling about 1.35 million square feet. Job Filing Number: BD23028760001B001 📊 New Building: The second-largest permit was $95 million for 114 Northeast 22nd Street in Miami, where New Sense LLC, an affiliate of Argentina-based HA Emprendimientos, is developing […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Swire Properties received $240M permit for Mandarin Oriental Brickell Key (1.35M SF, condos + hotel), Miami.
HA Emprendimientos/New Sense LLC secured $95M permit for Sense 22 Live Local project (320+ units, 36 stories, 503K SF); landed $111M construction loan.
South Florida issued $420M+ in construction permits week of July 22; Lore/Element and Swire driving mixed-use development.
stories.summary: South Florida issued over $420 million in construction permits for the week ending July 22, 2026. The largest was a $240 million permit for Swire Properties' Mandarin Oriental Brickell Key redevelopment (1.35 million square feet, condos and hotel, on former hotel site demolished in April). The second-largest was a $95 million permit for New Sense LLC's Sense 22 Live Local mixed-use project in Miami (503,000 square feet, 36 stories, 320+ units), which landed a separate $111 million construction loan from S3 Capital. Additional permits include Lore Development/Element's mixed-use project and the Miami Administration Building.
stories.impact: South Florida's permit velocity and construction loan availability signal robust capital access and strong multifamily demand. Multiple large permits in same week indicate competitive market and rapid entitlement processing. However, the sheer scale of permits ($420M+) suggests supply acceleration; monitor absorption rates and rent trends in Miami/Brickell in 2027–2029 when these units stabilize. Concurrent hotel and condo development indicates strong visitor/owner-occupancy demand, but conversion to rental (if market softens) could create supply overhang. S3 Capital's $111M construction commitment to Sense 22 underscores confidence in Live Local incentive economics but raises risk if incentives expire or rent growth stalls.

stories.title: UrbanStreet targets former Motorola office for residential conversion
stories.excerpt: UrbanStreet Group is planning a residential conversion of an atrium office building that used to house Motorola, as part of Schaumburg’s the District at Veridian development. During an informal presentation with village officials, UrbanStreet managing partner Bob Burk said that converting the six-story, 360,200-square-foot building would be a “poster child” for redevelopment creativity, according to the Chicago Daily Herald. The project also includes the construction of a sister building that would reach as high as four stories, giving space for the complex to have around 300 units. 18 row houses will connect the complex to D.R. Horton’s Northgate at Veridian […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: UrbanStreet planning 300-unit residential conversion of 360K-SF former Motorola office in Schaumburg as part of District at Veridian.
Project includes new sister four-story building, 18 row houses; phase one (nine-story apt) nearing construction completion by early 2027.
UrbanStreet financed phase one with $94M loan from Bank OZK; total phase one cost $175M on 225-acre former Motorola campus.
stories.summary: UrbanStreet Group is planning a residential conversion of a six-story, 360,200-square-foot office building that formerly housed Motorola in Schaumburg, Illinois, as part of the broader District at Veridian megadevelopment. The project includes approximately 300 units, a companion four-story new-build, and 18 row houses connecting to D.R. Horton's adjacent Northgate development. UrbanStreet purchased the Motorola campus a decade ago and broke ground on the $1 billion District at Veridian last fall. Phase one financing came via a $94 million construction loan from Bank OZK; phase one cost is pegged at $175 million. The first nine-story apartment building is nearing completion by early 2027.
stories.impact: Schaumburg's large-scale conversion and mixed-use redevelopment signals investor appetite for suburban office-to-residential conversion outside core metros like NYC and SF. The deal validates Bank OZK's willingness to finance Midwest conversion and underscores institutional buyer confidence in Schaumburg's rents and demographics. However, the $175M phase one cost on a mixed-use project suggests higher conversion economics than typical office-only deals; developers must carefully underwrite construction complexity, structural costs, and phasing to avoid cost overruns. Monitor absorption of phase one (completing 2027) to gauge renter demand in Schaumburg and inform subsequent phases.

stories.title: As Deerfield loosens waterfront development rules, West Palm tightens them
stories.excerpt: Two South Florida coastal cities are moving in opposite directions on waterfront development, as localities grapple with regulating construction on the region’s most valuable real estate.  Deerfield Beach is considering looser guidelines for building on the waterfront, while West Palm Beach has temporarily reined in development.  Deerfield Beach commissioners voted 3-2 Tuesday for charter amendments that would loosen decadeslong restrictions, including eliminating floor area ratio maximums for commercial and multifamily properties between the Intracoastal Waterway and the Atlantic Ocean, the South Florida Business Journal reported. The change also would lift limits on mixed-use developments and projects with accessory use that […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Deerfield Beach commissioners voted 3-2 to loosen waterfront building restrictions; eliminated FAR caps for commercial/multifamily between Intracoastal and Atlantic.
West Palm Beach simultaneously tightened waterfront development, restricting growth and creating regulatory divergence across South Florida coast.
Deerfield's changes face final vote Aug. 4 and referendum Nov. 3; height and lot coverage limits remain; voter approval requirement for $750K+ property sales eliminated.
stories.summary: Deerfield Beach commissioners voted 3-2 to loosen decades-long waterfront development restrictions via charter amendments, including eliminating floor area ratio (FAR) maximums for commercial and multifamily properties between the Intracoastal Waterway and Atlantic Ocean. The changes also lift limits on mixed-use developments and accessory uses. Height and lot coverage caps remain in place, and the voter approval requirement for city property sales over $750,000 would be eliminated. A final vote is scheduled for August 4, with a referendum on November 3. Meanwhile, West Palm Beach has temporarily tightened waterfront development, creating a regulatory divergence.
stories.impact: Deerfield's deregulation creates a competitive opportunity for multifamily developers and will likely accelerate waterfront and near-waterfront supply if approved and upheld in referendum. Potential FAR removal could drive higher-density multifamily in premium waterfront locations, increasing supply and moderating rents in Deerfield. However, West Palm Beach's tightening creates bifurcated supply dynamics: West Palm may see constrained growth and higher rents, while Deerfield absorbs spill-over demand. Monitor Deerfield referendum closely; if passed, expect increased entitlement activity and construction starts in 2027–2028, pressuring Deerfield area rents. West Palm's tightening may support sustained rent growth and values for existing properties and reduce new supply competition.

stories.title: Alonzo Mourning, HTG net $55M financing package for affordable housing
stories.excerpt: NBA hall-of-famer Alonzo Mourning and Housing Trust Group scored a $55.4 million financing package for a Naranja affordable housing complex. The deal comes as the duo has racked up financing in recent years for below-market rate rentals in Miami-Dade County.  Mourning, through his AM Affordable Housing, and Coconut Grove-based Housing Trust Group plan the 115-unit Artisan Pointe project at 26115 South Dixie Highway in an unincorporated area of south Miami-Dade County. The four-story complex is under construction with completion expected in January 2028, according to the developers’ news release.  The financing package includes $31.5 million in low-income housing tax credit […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Alonzo Mourning (AM Affordable Housing) and Housing Trust Group closed $55.4M financing for 115-unit Artisan Pointe, Naranja, south Miami-Dade.
Financing: $31.5M LIHTC equity, $16.9M Berkadia permanent loan, $7M Naranja CRA funds (tax-increment + loan); units restricted to 30–70% AMI.
Under construction; delivery January 2028; one-bed (30% AMI) = $766/month; two-bed (70% AMI) = $2,145/month vs. area median rent of $2,277.
stories.summary: NBA Hall-of-Famer Alonzo Mourning's AM Affordable Housing and Housing Trust Group secured $55.4 million in financing for Artisan Pointe, a 115-unit affordable housing project in unincorporated Naranja, south Miami-Dade County. The financing package includes $31.5 million in low-income housing tax credit equity, a $16.9 million permanent loan arranged by Berkadia, and $7 million from Naranja Lakes Community Redevelopment Agency (split between tax-increment funding and a loan). The four-story project is under construction with delivery expected January 2028. Rents are restricted to households earning 30%, 60%, and 70% of AMI, with one-bedrooms at 30% AMI renting at $766/month and two-bedrooms at 70% AMI at $2,145/month.
stories.impact: Artisan Pointe demonstrates strong institutional and public sector appetite for affordable multifamily in Miami-Dade, with stable LIHTC and CRA/TIF capital flows. Mourning's brand and HTG's track record de-risk the deal and attract patient capital. However, the project's affordability restriction (30–70% AMI rents of $766–$2,145) is substantially below area median rent ($2,277), requiring subsidy to pencil and limiting market-rate development feasibility in Naranja. CRA and TIF funds are finite; if increasingly allocated to affordable projects, market-rate development in Naranja may slow. Monitor whether HTG's announced master-plan phases get funded; if so, Naranja becomes an affordable-dominated submarket, reducing market-rate developer interest.
